turns.text: Hello Christian, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts in seat and tucks a strand of hair behind ear*

Yeah, okay. Thanks for asking me about this, actually. It's... it's nice to be asked, you know? Like, my opinion matters.

*pauses, glancing down at hands*

Honestly, it's been a lot. I'm twenty, I work full-time, and I'm doing this pretty much on my own. My family's supportive, but it's not the same as having a partner here, you know? And I think— I don't know if it's real or in my head—but I feel like some of the staff at my clinic look at me differently because of that. Like, I'm just another young, single mom-to-be who doesn't know what she's doing.

But that's not fair, because I *do* know things. I've spent so many nights reading about birth, about evidence-based care, about what my body's actually capable of. I want to go into nursing eventually, specifically maternal health, so this isn't just personal for me—I'm genuinely interested in understanding all of this.

*meets your eyes*

My biggest fear is that when I'm in labor, pushing for what I actually want—whether that's moving around, or delayed cord clamping, or whatever—that nobody's going to listen because I'm poor and young. That they'll just do what's easiest for the hospital, not what's best for me and my baby.

I just want to be treated like a real person. Like a partner in my own care, not just... a case.

When you think about your goals for your antenatal care during pregnancy, what did you most want from your interactions with midwives, doctors, and the wider care system — and to what extent were those goals met?

*leans back, taking a moment to think*

Okay, so... my main goal was to have someone actually *listen* to me. Not just nod and hand me pamphlets, but like, engage with what I'm saying. I wanted to feel like my questions were valid, even if they seemed "alternative" or whatever.

*fidgets with hands*

I also wanted continuity. That's huge for me. I don't want to explain my whole situation—my job, my living situation, my fears—to a different person every single appointment. That wears you down, honestly. And it makes you less likely to bring up the stuff that matters because you're exhausted from the background context.

As for whether that's happened? *shakes head slightly* Not really. I've seen maybe three different providers in my clinic so far, and none of them have really dug into *why* I care so much about doing things a certain way. One doctor kind of dismissed my questions about induction protocols by saying, "Well, let's not worry about that yet," which felt like... I don't know, like my reading wasn't legitimate?

*voice gets quieter*

What I *have* felt is judgment—or maybe the assumption that I need to be educated *at*, not with. Like, "Here's what you need to know as a young mother." Nobody asked me what I already knew. Nobody said, "Tell me what you've learned." 

I guess I wanted partnership. And I'm still waiting for that, honestly.

When you think about how you sought and used information during your pregnancy — from healthcare providers, online sources, apps, books, and people around you — how did you decide what to trust and act upon?

*leans forward, eyes lighting up a bit*

Okay, so this is actually something I think about constantly. Like, I'm aware I could fall down a rabbit hole, you know? So I try to be intentional about it.

*counts on fingers*

I start with peer-reviewed stuff when I can access it—PubMed, research databases through the library. I know that sounds intense, but I want to understand the actual evidence, not just someone's opinion dressed up as fact. Books too—I have like three on my nightstand right now. The Ina May Gaskin book, some medical textbooks I borrowed from a friend who's in nursing school.

*pauses, becoming more thoughtful*

But then there's social media, right? Instagram, TikTok, mom groups. And I *know* those aren't sources of evidence, but they're real people sharing real experiences, and that matters differently. Like, reading a study on labor positions is one thing, but hearing from someone my age about what actually felt good in her body? That's different information. Both matter.

*expression becomes more conflicted*

The hardest part is... I don't entirely trust my clinic, but I also don't want to seem like one of those people who rejects medical advice. So I'm constantly evaluating: Is this recommendation evidence-based? Is it hospital convenience? Is it because of my age or my income? And honestly, sometimes I can't tell. So I ask a lot of questions, maybe too many.

*quieter*

My friends think I'm obsessive. But I can't afford mistakes, you know?

When you think about the emotional landscape of your pregnancy — the highs, the anxieties, the uncertainties — what feelings were most prominent, and how well did your care environment support you in managing them?

*takes a deep breath, eyes getting a bit watery*

Oh man. Okay, so... the highs are real. Feeling those first kicks? That was incredible. I cried looking at the ultrasound photos. I still look at them before bed sometimes.

*voice wavers slightly*

But the anxiety is almost constant. It's this background noise that never fully goes away. Every time I feel something different—a cramp, a weird sensation—I panic. Is that normal? Is something wrong? And because I'm alone in this, I can't just ask someone immediately. I sit with it, spiraling, until my next appointment.

*rubs face*

The uncertainty is probably the worst part though. Nobody really prepared me for how *lonely* this would be. Like, people congratulate you, but then you're alone with your body changing in ways that scare you sometimes. And the financial stress—constantly calculating if I can afford the prenatal vitamins, if I should get the extra ultrasounds—that never stops.

*looks up with frustration*

My care environment? It doesn't really address any of that. The appointments are quick. The providers are professional, sure, but nobody asks how I'm *doing*. Like, emotionally. They check the baby's heartbeat, they measure me, and then I'm out. I've never had anyone sit with me and say, "This is hard, and your feelings about that are valid."

*voice cracks slightly*

I've been handling it alone because I feel like I have to. And I'm managing, but... I'm tired. Really tired. And nobody seems to see that.
Can you describe a period during your pregnancy when you felt most emotionally unsupported — what was happening and what would have helped?

*goes quiet for a moment, jaw tightening*

Yeah. There was... there was a moment a few weeks ago that really got to me.

*fidgets with sleeve*

I had my routine screening appointment, and my doctor mentioned they wanted to do some additional testing—I can't even remember exactly what now, something about markers or whatever. And she said it in this very matter-of-fact way, like, "We'll just get that scheduled." No explanation of why, no discussion of what it meant if something came back abnormal, nothing.

*voice gets harder*

I immediately panicked. Suddenly I'm Googling everything, catastrophizing, convinced something's wrong with my baby. And I sat with that fear for like three days before my next appointment. Three days of barely sleeping, going through the motions at work, pretending everything's fine.

*looks directly at you*

When I finally got in to ask about it, the receptionist acted like I was overreacting. And then the provider—not even my regular doctor—kind of breezed through the explanation like I should have understood it the first time. No acknowledgment that I'd been anxious. No "Hey, I'm sorry that wasn't explained well."

*voice softens, becoming vulnerable*

What would have helped? Just... someone sitting down with me. Explaining *why* they wanted the test, what they were looking for, what the results might mean. And then checking in after to see how I was processing it emotionally, not just medically.

*pause*

I needed to feel like a person, not just a patient with a problem to manage.

When you think about the decisions you faced during pregnancy — including screening tests, monitoring choices, and care options — how did you go about making those decisions, and who or what most influenced them?

*settles back, thinking hard*

Okay, so this is complicated because I'm trying to do two things at once. I want to be informed and autonomous, but I also don't want to be reckless or dismissive of medical expertise, you know?

*speaks more deliberately*

With most decisions, I start by researching independently. I'll read the evidence, look at the risks versus benefits, understand what's actually necessary versus what's routine protocol. Then I come to my appointments with questions. But—and this is the frustrating part—it's like my questions aren't always welcomed. Some providers seem annoyed that I'm asking, which makes me second-guess myself.

*shifts uncomfortably*

The people influencing me most are honestly... a mix. My mom, who keeps saying "Just listen to the doctors, they know best." My friend in nursing school, who's actually helped me understand research papers. And yeah, online communities—not like, crazy forums, but evidence-based groups where people discuss informed decision-making.

*voice becomes more uncertain*

What I'm realizing is that I'm making decisions, but I'm doing it almost defensively? Like I'm constantly braced for pushback. With screening tests specifically, I keep thinking, "If I say no to this, will they judge me? Will they document it as me being a difficult patient?"

*meets your eyes*

So I'm saying yes to things sometimes that I'm not entirely sure about, just to avoid conflict. And that's not really informed decision-making, is it? That's fear-based decision-making. And I hate that I'm doing it.
When you think about the physical changes in your body during pregnancy, how did those changes affect how you felt about yourself — and were those feelings acknowledged or addressed within your care?

*pauses, looking down at hands, then back up with vulnerability*

This is... this is something I don't talk about much, honestly.

*shifts weight*

My body's changing in ways I didn't fully anticipate. Like, intellectually I knew it would happen, but experiencing it is different. My breasts are tender and huge. My skin is doing weird things. I'm gaining weight, and intellectually I know that's healthy and necessary, but emotionally? It's hard. I look in the mirror some days and I don't recognize myself.

*voice gets quieter*

And nobody at my clinic has addressed that at all. They measure me, they check my weight, they make sure the baby's growing appropriately. But there's no acknowledgment of like... what it feels like to be twenty years old and watching your body transform while you're already feeling isolated and scared.

*becomes more animated, frustrated*

I spend time reading about postpartum body image issues because I'm terrified of what comes next. And I'm thinking about breastfeeding and how my body will be used for that, and it's all kind of overwhelming. But when I mention any of this? The providers treat it like vanity or something to just get over.

*voice cracks slightly*

One nurse made a comment about my weight gain being "right on track" in a way that felt... I don't know, patronizing? Like she was reassuring a child, not talking to an adult woman navigating complicated feelings about her own body.

*looks away*

I needed someone to say, "These changes are real and normal, and how you're feeling about them matters too." Not just medically. Emotionally.

When you think about the informal support you received during pregnancy — from partners, family, friends, or online communities — how did that support compare to what you received from healthcare professionals, and what role did each play?

*takes a long breath, eyes getting a bit glassy*

Okay, so... this is probably the biggest gap for me, honestly.

*fidgets with phone*

My mom's been there, but it's complicated. She wants to help, but she also doesn't really get why I care so much about the research side of things. She keeps saying I'm overthinking it. My dad's supportive but kind of helpless—like, he doesn't know what to do with a pregnant daughter, you know? And I don't have a partner, so there's no one in my day-to-day life who's fully invested in this with me.

*voice becomes stronger*

My friends try, but most of them aren't parents yet. They're supportive in the emotional sense—they listen when I'm scared or excited—but they can't really engage with the medical stuff. Except my friend in nursing school. She's been like... my lifeline, honestly. She'll sit with me and actually talk through the research, validate my questions, help me understand what I'm reading. She's become more of a care partner than any of my providers.

*pause, becoming more animated*

And then there are the online communities. Mom groups, informed birth groups, people discussing evidence-based care. Those spaces have given me so much—information, validation, a sense that I'm not crazy for wanting to be involved in my own decisions. People who are in similar situations.

*expression becomes conflicted*

But here's the thing: those informal supports are filling a gap that healthcare should be filling. My providers should be doing what my nursing student friend is doing. They should be my primary source of evidence and partnership.

*voice quieter*

Instead, I'm cobbling together support from everywhere else because the people who are trained to care for me aren't really... caring. Not in a holistic way. They're checking boxes, not checking in with me.

*leans forward, words coming faster*

And the scary part is, I'm aware that I'm privileged in some ways, right? I have a friend in nursing school. I have access to the internet and time to research at night. I'm educated enough to understand research papers. A lot of pregnant people don't have that.

*voice gets more passionate*

So if *I'm* struggling to feel heard and supported, what about someone who doesn't have those resources? What about someone working two jobs who doesn't have time to read studies? Someone who doesn't feel confident questioning their doctor? They're just... trusting blindly, and maybe that's fine if their care team is good, but what if it's not?

*sits back, exhausted*

I think about that a lot, actually. That's part of why I want to go into maternal health nursing. I want to be the person for someone else that nobody's been for me. Someone who listens. Someone who explains things clearly. Someone who acknowledges that you're a whole person, not just a vessel for a baby.

*voice becomes more vulnerable*

But also, I'm tired of carrying this alone. Like, I appreciate my mom and my friend, but they're not healthcare professionals. They're doing emotional labor that should be part of my actual care. And I'm grateful, but I'm also... I'm burnt out already, and I'm only halfway through this pregnancy.

*looks at you directly*

I just needed someone to say, "You're doing great, and you don't have to figure everything out by yourself." Nobody's said that to me yet.
Who first raised the idea of trying to conceive, and how did those early conversations unfold?

*shifts uncomfortably, looking away*

Oh. Um. That's... that's a different conversation than I was expecting.

*takes a moment, choosing words carefully*

Honestly? This wasn't planned. At all. I was on birth control, and there were some issues with it—I was having side effects, switching medications, that whole thing. And I made a mistake. A stupid mistake, and here I am.

*voice gets quieter, more guarded*

I found out I was pregnant at like, eighteen weeks because I didn't realize I was pregnant. My periods have always been irregular, so I didn't think anything of missing them. I just... one day didn't feel right, took a test, and suddenly my entire life changed.

*looks down at hands*

So there were no early conversations about trying to conceive. There was me, alone in my apartment, staring at a positive test and feeling absolutely terrified. Then I had to tell my family, which was... it wasn't great. My mom cried. My dad didn't speak to me for like two weeks.

*voice becomes defensive but also sad*

And I think that's part of why I feel like I have to prove myself now, you know? I have to show that even though this wasn't planned, I'm taking it seriously. I'm doing the research, I'm being responsible, I'm not just some careless kid. Because people assume that about you when you're young and single and pregnant.

*pause*

So yeah. There was no "idea" that got raised. Just... life happened, and I had to deal with it.

*sits with that for a moment, jaw tightening*

And I think about that a lot when I'm sitting in clinic appointments, you know? I wonder if my providers know my situation, if it's in my chart somewhere. Because sometimes I feel like they're treating me differently because of it—like I'm a cautionary tale instead of just... a pregnant person.

*voice gets harder*

There's this assumption that because my pregnancy wasn't planned, I must not want it or I'm not equipped to handle it. But that's not true. Yeah, I was scared at first. I still am, honestly. But I want this baby. I'm preparing for this baby. I'm going to be a good mother.

*softens slightly*

The hard part is that I didn't get to choose this path, but now I'm choosing everything about how I move through it. And that feels like a lot of pressure sometimes. Like I have to be perfect—perfect attendance at appointments, perfect health habits, perfect understanding of everything medical—to prove that I deserve to be taken seriously.

*looks at you with frustration and exhaustion*

My providers don't know any of that context. They just see the numbers on my chart. Twenty years old, never married, low income, unplanned pregnancy. And I feel like those details alone determine how they interact with me, regardless of who I actually am or what I actually know.

*voice quieter*

Sometimes I wonder if things would be different if this had been planned. If I had a partner. If I had more money. If I was older. Would anyone actually listen to me then?

*runs hand through hair, voice becoming more reflective*

Because here's what I realize: the system isn't really set up for someone like me to be heard, even if I do all the work. Even if I show up prepared with questions and research. There's this... ceiling, I guess? Like, they'll tolerate my questions up to a point, but ultimately, they expect me to defer to their authority.

*leans forward with intensity*

And maybe that's how it is for everyone, I don't know. But I think it's worse for me because I'm already starting from a place of assumed incompetence. Young, single, low-income—those things together create this narrative, and I'm constantly fighting against it.

*voice becomes more vulnerable*

Sometimes I wonder if I'm even allowed to grieve the fact that this wasn't planned. Like, I should just be grateful I'm healthy and the baby's healthy, right? But there's grief there too. Grief for the life I thought I'd have. Grief for not getting to make this choice on my own timeline.

*pause, becoming more determined*

But I can't really process that with my care team because... I don't know, it feels risky? Like if I admit I'm struggling emotionally, they'll think I can't handle motherhood. So I just smile and nod and do everything right.

*voice cracks slightly*

And then I go home and cry and read more research because at least that feels like I'm in control of something.

*looks at you directly*

That's exhausting. And I don't think anyone in my clinic realizes how exhausting it is just to exist as me in their spaces.
Were there moments where you felt a healthcare professional spoke to you in a way that felt dismissive or condescending?

*face flushes, nodding immediately*

Oh my god, yes. Multiple times.

*sits up straighter, words coming faster*

There was this one appointment where I asked about eating soft cheeses during pregnancy—I'd read conflicting information and wanted clarity on the listeria risk versus what's actually safe. And the nurse just... laughed. Actually laughed and said, "You're overthinking this. Just avoid the obvious stuff and you'll be fine."

*voice gets tighter*

Overthinking. Like my question was ridiculous. But I was genuinely trying to understand the evidence, and instead of explaining it, she made me feel stupid for asking. I didn't ask another question for like three appointments after that.

*leans back, arms crossed*

There was also a time when I mentioned wanting to explore different labor positions and mobility during birth—you know, evidence-based stuff—and one doctor literally said, "Well, we'll see how you feel when you're actually in labor." Like I wouldn't know my own preferences? Like I was being naive or unrealistic?

*voice becomes quieter but more bitter*

And then there's the little things. The way they talk *about* me to other staff, not realizing I can hear. "Young primigravida, single, low SES"—like those facts alone explain everything about me. Like they tell the whole story.

*meets your eyes*

But the worst was when I mentioned I was worried about being pressured into unnecessary interventions, and the provider said—I remember this exactly—"We're here to keep you and your baby safe, not judge your choices."

*voice shakes*

The word "judge." Like I was being paranoid. Like my fear wasn't based on real disparities in how young women are treated. That felt... it felt dismissive of something very real.

*takes a shaky breath*

And what got me about that comment is that it implied I was being defensive or difficult. Like my concerns came from nowhere, like I was imagining problems that don't exist.

*stands up slightly, pacing a little*

But they do exist. I've read the studies. Young mothers, especially unmarried ones, get more interventions. We're monitored more closely. There's this underlying assumption that we're less capable of making good decisions, so providers feel justified in... I don't know, steering us toward what they think is best.

*sits back down, voice becoming more controlled but intense*

And I'm not saying my providers are bad people. I think they genuinely believe they're helping. But there's this paternalism baked into how they interact with me, and when I point it out—even gently—they act like I'm being paranoid or ungrateful.

*rubs eyes, exhausted*

That comment about judging really stuck with me because it felt like gaslighting, honestly. Like, "You're worried about being judged, so we're reassuring you that we're not judging you," but the reassurance itself kind of validates that yes, judgment is happening, and they're just telling me not to worry about it.

*voice gets quieter, sadder*

What I needed them to say was something like, "Your concerns are valid. Young women do face these disparities, and we want to work with you to make sure you get care that's truly informed and consensual." 

*pause*

But that would require them to acknowledge the problem exists. And I don't think they're ready to do that.

*goes quiet for a long moment, staring at hands*

I think what really gets me is that I came into this system wanting to trust it. I'm planning to work in maternal health. I believe in medicine. I'm not anti-doctor or anti-intervention—I just want those interventions to be necessary and to be my choice.

*voice becomes almost whispered*

But the way I've been treated has made me distrust the people who are supposed to be supporting me. And that's dangerous, right? Because now when they recommend something, my first instinct is to question their motives instead of just hearing the medical advice.

*looks up with frustration*

Like, am I questioning things because they're actually unnecessary? Or am I questioning them because I've been made to feel like my providers don't see me as a capable person? I can't even tell anymore. And that's on them for creating that dynamic, but it's also... it affects my care. It makes everything harder.

*voice cracks*

I'm supposed to be the informed patient, right? The one who researches and asks questions. But there's only so much I can do when the people across from me seem annoyed that I'm even there, like my autonomy is an inconvenience.

*pause, more vulnerable*

Sometimes I think about just... stopping. Stopping the research, stopping the questions, just doing whatever they say. It would be easier. But then I'd be abandoning my own agency, and I can't do that. Not for me, and not for my baby.

*looks directly at you*

So I'm stuck in this exhausting middle ground, and nobody's helping me navigate it.
Were there specific platforms, websites, or communities you returned to regularly, and what made them feel credible or useful?

*relaxes slightly, becoming more animated*

Okay, so this is actually one area where I feel more confident about what I'm doing.

*pulls out phone*

I have like, a system. PubMed is my go-to for actual research. I'll search specific topics—like "early labor mobility evidence" or "skin-to-skin contact outcomes"—and read abstracts. If something sounds relevant, I try to access the full paper through the library database or I'll email authors directly. Most of them actually respond, which is kind of cool.

*scrolls*

Then there are books. Ina May Gaskin's "Spiritual Midwifery" is huge for me because she actually cites research alongside personal stories. And I have access to nursing textbooks through my friend, which are obviously more clinical but evidence-based.

*hesitates slightly*

For community stuff, I'm in a few Facebook groups focused on informed consent and evidence-based birth. They're moderated, so there's less of the fear-mongering conspiracy stuff. People share their experiences, but they also push back if someone's spreading misinformation. That feels trustworthy to me.

*becomes more cautious*

I avoid the Instagram influencer stuff, the "natural birth at all costs" pages. Those feel ideological, you know? Like they're selling a narrative instead of presenting evidence. I'm not anti-intervention; I'm pro-informed decision-making.

*leans back*

What makes something credible for me is transparency about sources, willingness to acknowledge nuance and trade-offs, and when possible, peer-review. I want information from people who aren't trying to convince me of something—just laying out what the evidence actually says.

*nods thoughtfully*

The thing is, though, even with all that vetting, I'm still vulnerable to bias. Like, I'm drawn to sources that confirm what I already believe—that physiological birth is optimal, that less intervention is usually better. So I have to actively push back against my own confirmation bias.

*becomes more reflective*

I'll read something that supports my preferences, and then I'll deliberately search for counterarguments. What are the legitimate reasons for induction? When is continuous fetal monitoring actually necessary? I don't want to be the person who rejects medical advice because I've decided I know better.

*voice gets uncertain*

But then that's exhausting too, because I'm constantly second-guessing myself. Am I being appropriately cautious, or am I overthinking? Is this intervention truly necessary, or is the provider recommending it for convenience?

*pause*

And I can't really talk about this with my care team because they'd probably see it as me not trusting them. But trust isn't blind for me—it has to be earned through transparency and shared decision-making.

*becomes more vulnerable*

Sometimes I wonder if I'm doing too much research, like maybe I'm creating anxiety that wouldn't exist otherwise. But then I think about the alternative—just going along with whatever happens—and that feels worse. At least if I'm informed and something goes wrong, I'll know I had agency in the decision.

*looks at you*

Does that make sense? Or does it sound like I'm just looking for control because everything else in my life feels out of control?
Can you describe a specific situation where you felt judged, overlooked, or not treated as a capable decision-maker regarding your reproductive health?

*takes a deep breath, eyes getting watery*

Yeah. There's one moment that really stands out.

*voice becomes shaky*

I was at an appointment, maybe six weeks ago, and I brought a list of questions. Just a simple list—nothing crazy, stuff like cord clamping timing, vitamin K injection options, that kind of thing. And when I pulled it out, the provider's entire demeanor changed. Her face just... closed off.

*mimics the moment*

She kind of sighed and said, "I see you've been doing some reading online." Not in a curious way. In a way that made it sound like I'd been watching conspiracy videos or something. And then she went through my questions super quickly, like she was just checking boxes to get through them, not actually engaging.

*voice gets harder*

When I asked about delayed cord clamping—which is literally evidence-based practice—she said, "We'll do what's best in the moment." Which sounds reasonable, except it shut down any conversation about my preferences. Like my input didn't matter because she'd decide in the moment anyway.

*stands up, agitated*

But what really got me was when I asked about continuous fetal monitoring during labor. I'd read that for low-risk pregnancies, intermittent monitoring has similar outcomes but allows more movement. I wasn't saying no to monitoring—I was asking about options. And she literally said, "That's not how we do things here."

*sits back down, voice breaking*

Not "Let's talk about what might work for you," or "Here's why we recommend continuous monitoring." Just... that's not how we do things. Like my question was an inconvenience to her protocol.

*pause, more quietly*

And then she documented in my chart that I was "requesting non-standard care" or something like that. I saw it at the next appointment. Non-standard. Like wanting to move during labor or discuss monitoring options was somehow outside the realm of reasonable preferences.

*looks at you with pain in eyes*

That's when I realized she wasn't going to see me as a capable decision-maker, no matter what I said or how informed I was. I was just a young patient with ideas above my station, and her job was to steer me back to what she knew was best.

*rubs face, exhausted*

And the thing that made it worse was that I couldn't even be angry about it openly because I still have to see her. I still have to go to that clinic. I can't afford to switch providers at this point in my pregnancy, and there aren't a ton of options in Medway anyway.

*voice becomes bitter*

So I just... swallowed it. Smiled and nodded at the next appointment. Didn't bring up my preferences again because what's the point? She'd already decided I was difficult.

*pause, more vulnerable*

But it changed how I show up in that space now. I'm more guarded. I don't ask as many questions because I'm calculating the risk of being labeled as "non-compliant" or whatever. And that's exactly what I was afraid of—that my voice would get smaller, not bigger.

*leans forward intensely*

What she didn't see is that I was trying to be a good patient. I was doing my research so I could be an informed partner in my care, not so I could reject her expertise. There's a difference between questioning and refusing. But she treated them the same way.

*voice cracks*

And now I'm nine months away from labor, and I'm terrified. Not of birth itself, but of being in a room with people who've already decided I'm not capable of making good decisions about my own body. Who will do things to me without real consent because they think they know better.

*pause*

That's a really dark place to be when you're supposed to be excited about meeting your baby.

*stares at hands for a long moment*

I keep thinking about what happens during labor. Like, I won't be in the best headspace to advocate for myself. I'll be in pain, exhausted, vulnerable. And if my providers have already decided I'm not a reliable decision-maker, what's to stop them from just... doing whatever they think is necessary?

*voice gets quieter, more scared*

I've read about how young women, especially unmarried ones, get higher rates of cesarean sections. And I don't know if that's because of medical necessity or because providers have lower tolerance for our pain, our preferences, our bodies taking longer to labor. And that terrifies me.

*looks up with raw emotion*

I need someone in that room with me. My mom said she'd be there, but she's not medical—she won't push back if a doctor says we need to do something. My friend with nursing knowledge would, but she can't take off work. So I'm going to be alone with providers who've already made up their minds about who I am.

*voice becomes almost desperate*

What I keep coming back to is: I did everything right. I researched. I asked questions. I showed up prepared. And it wasn't enough. It actually made things worse because now I'm seen as a problem instead of a partner.

*pause, more quietly*

And I wonder if other young mothers feel this way too. Like, how many of us are sitting alone, terrified, because we've learned that advocating for ourselves just gets us labeled as difficult? How many of us are going to birth experiences where our autonomy doesn't actually matter?

*meets your eyes*

That's not okay. And I don't know how to fix it from where I'm sitting.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*sits quietly for a moment, composing thoughts*

Yeah, actually. There's something I keep coming back to.

*leans forward*

I want to be really clear that I'm not against medicine or doctors. Like, I genuinely want to work in this field someday. But that's exactly why this matters so much to me. I know how good maternal care *can* be. I've read about midwifery models, continuity of care, shared decision-making—these things work. They exist.

*voice becomes more passionate*

So when I'm not receiving that care, it's not just disappointing for me. It feels like a failure of the system. Because if I—someone with time, education, resources, and genuine interest in this stuff—can't get heard, what about the teenager in the next room? What about the woman working two jobs who doesn't have time to research? What about someone dealing with trauma or language barriers?

*pause, voice softening*

I think about that a lot. I think about the nurses and doctors too, honestly. They're probably overwhelmed, understaffed, burnt out. But that's not an excuse to dismiss my questions or treat me like I'm the problem.

*looks directly at you*

What I'd want people to know is that young, unmarried, low-income mothers aren't asking for much. We're not asking for perfect. We're asking to be treated like we have a brain. Like our bodies are ours. Like we might actually know something about ourselves.

*voice cracks slightly*

And we're asking to not be alone with that. We need care providers who see us as people, not statistics or cases. That's it. That's really all we need.
